jokes.text: Good grief, This candy's terrible. Circus peanuts, raisins, nicotine gum, a library card...
jokes.setup: Kid complaining about bad Halloween candy
jokes.punchline: Library card as the worst 'candy' - escalation from bad candy to non-candy
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons escalation - starts with genuinely bad candy and ends with something that's not candy at all. Good economy and character voice.

jokes.text: You got all the good stuff! - And yet and I'm still not satisfied.
jokes.setup: Lisa sarcastically saying Bart got 'good stuff'
jokes.punchline: Bart's deadpan admission of still being unsatisfied despite having terrible candy
jokes.explanation: Simple irony joke - works but not particularly memorable or clever.

jokes.text: You thieving hussy!
jokes.setup: Marge catching someone taking candy
jokes.punchline: Marge using unexpectedly harsh language ('hussy') for a minor offense
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Marge using old-fashioned dramatic language for something trivial. High quotability.

jokes.text: Eat fire! Punks! What the... I'm still cold.
jokes.setup: Homer dramatically threatening someone with fire
jokes.punchline: The anticlimactic revelation that whatever he's doing isn't working
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer moment - big dramatic gesture followed by mundane reality check.

jokes.text: Pathetic humans! They are showing a halloween episode... in November!
jokes.setup: Alien commenting on human behavior
jokes.punchline: Breaking fourth wall about the show's own late Halloween episode timing
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-joke that acknowledges the show's own scheduling while staying in character. Smart self-awareness without breaking the show's reality.

jokes.text: Oh, still thinking about halloween. We've already got our christmas decorations up.
jokes.setup: Following up the Halloween lateness complaint
jokes.punchline: Aliens have already moved on to Christmas - exaggerating human holiday rushing
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the meta-joke, commenting on holiday commercialization through alien perspective.

jokes.text: I AM DEATH. - Death? We don't want any.
jokes.setup: Death making a dramatic pronouncement
jokes.punchline: Homer treating Death like a door-to-door salesman
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - treating the most serious thing possible with casual dismissal. Excellent economy and character consistency.

jokes.text: Bart! Run like a wind(pronounced as waind)! - Mom. It's wind. Well, I only read it in books.
jokes.setup: Marge telling Bart to run, mispronouncing 'wind'
jokes.punchline: Marge's admission that she only knows the word from reading, explaining the mispronunciation
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Marge's limited worldly experience in a specific, believable way.

jokes.text: Please don't take me! Take Milhouse! We all know there's no happy ending there.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to avoid death
jokes.punchline: Casually throwing Milhouse under the bus with dark logic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart selfishness combined with dark humor about Milhouse's perpetual misfortune.

jokes.text: Does this mean I'll never cancel the Jim Belushi show? - I guess so. - NOOOOOOOOOOOO!
jokes.setup: Homer realizing the implications of a world without death
jokes.punchline: His horror at never being able to cancel a bad TV show
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - of all the implications of immortality, Homer focuses on bad television. Great cultural reference that feels earned.

jokes.text: If I know I'll tell you, I swear! I'd better call my wife, tell her I'll be late.
jokes.setup: Gangster being tortured can't die
jokes.punchline: Treating eternal torture like being late for dinner
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic - domestic concerns during cosmic crisis

jokes.text: Pizza! There's money on the counter. No tip.
jokes.setup: Pizza delivery during what appears to be a mob interrogation/torture scene
jokes.punchline: The casual mundanity of the delivery amid violence, plus the no-tip cheapness
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist juxtaposition of mundane delivery service with criminal activity. The no-tip detail adds character.

jokes.text: Check it out! I finally found a dead guy's clothes that fits me!
jokes.setup: Homer trying on Death's robes
jokes.punchline: His excitement about finding clothes that fit, ignoring the morbid source
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer priorities - focusing on the practical benefit while missing the bigger picture.

jokes.text: I might occasionally kill out of anger, or to illustrate a point. But I am not a Grim Reaper...
jokes.setup: Homer protesting that he won't be the Grim Reaper
jokes.punchline: His casual admission that he might kill for petty reasons
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - the distinction between professional and casual murder. Excellent character consistency and dark humor.

jokes.text: Where's doug? Never mind. I'm death now. I liked doug.
jokes.setup: Jasper asking about regular Death
jokes.punchline: Accepts new Death casually after mild preference for predecessor
jokes.explanation: Nice elderly character reaction - polite disappointment with change

jokes.text: He performs a valuable service. But it's often misunderstood. Like a vulture or the flesh eating maggot.
jokes.setup: Lisa defending her father's new job as Death
jokes.punchline: Comparing death to increasingly grotesque but 'valuable' creatures
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa moment - her earnest intellectualism applied to defending something absurd. Nice escalation in the comparisons.

jokes.text: Pardon me. Coming through. Rest in peace. You're dead. Take a dirt nap. Meet the worms.
jokes.setup: Homer working his way through a crowded venue as Death
jokes.punchline: Casual death euphemisms delivered like 'excuse me' as he passes people
jokes.explanation: Clever escalation of politeness mixed with death. The casual delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Ok. Who am I giving the finger to today? Marge Simpson... What was that first one again?
jokes.setup: Homer checking his death list casually
jokes.punchline: Not registering that his wife is on the list, then asking to repeat it
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's obliviousness even in extreme situations.

jokes.text: Hey. Wait a minute. This isn't Marge. This is her fat sister. Selma. - It's Patty. Chump!
jokes.setup: Homer realizing he got the wrong person
jokes.punchline: Getting the twin sisters confused and being corrected insultingly
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer/sisters dynamic. Works for character consistency but not particularly original.

jokes.text: So it's for what? My whole deal? - This is Professor John Frink, isn't it? - If I say no, do I still win?
jokes.setup: Frink assuming he won the Nobel Prize for everything he's done
jokes.punchline: His attempt to game the system when he realizes there might be a mistake
jokes.explanation: Good Frink moment showing his mixture of intelligence and desperation for recognition.

jokes.text: So it's for what? My whole deal? - This is Professor John Frink, isn't it? - If I say no, do I still win?
jokes.setup: Frink getting Nobel Prize call
jokes.punchline: Willing to lie about identity to keep prize
jokes.explanation: Good Frink character moment - nerdy but opportunistic

jokes.text: They must've awarded me for my invention of a hammer with a screw driver on the other end. It's convenient. - Just for that? - It was a slow year.
jokes.setup: Frink assuming his Nobel Prize is for something significant
jokes.punchline: It's for a simple combination tool, and it was a 'slow year'
jokes.explanation: Good satire of the Nobel Prize's prestige - won for something mundane in a 'slow year.'

jokes.text: Mother used to say we get along like positrons and anti-nutrinos. - Yeah, I'm geek.
jokes.setup: Frink using a complex physics analogy for family conflict
jokes.punchline: His immediate self-aware acknowledgment of his own nerdiness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frink character moment - using scientific terminology then immediately acknowledging his geekiness.

jokes.text: he was one of those Heman scientists who work on the atom bomb by day and slept with Marilyn Monroe by night and sold secrets to the Russians at lunch.
jokes.setup: Describing his father as a 'real man' scientist
jokes.punchline: Escalating list of stereotypical manly scientist activities ending with casual treason
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of macho scientist stereotypes, ending with the casual mention of treason 'at lunch.'

jokes.text: I get seesick taking a shower! Clean, but nauseous!
jokes.setup: Frink explaining why he can't go on an ocean expedition
jokes.punchline: Getting seasick from a shower, with the contradictory result
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frink absurdity - taking a normal weakness to an illogical extreme with precise description.

jokes.text: Does shark do that? - Yes. He was testing out a new blood-based suntan lotion.
jokes.setup: Question about whether sharks could completely devour someone
jokes.punchline: The absurd reason why he was particularly vulnerable to shark attack
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist explanation for the father's death - blood-based suntan lotion is perfectly illogical.

jokes.text: Except you have no penis. - In the traditional sense. - So what am I?
jokes.setup: Discussing what organs were replaced with machinery
jokes.punchline: The qualifier 'in the traditional sense' suggesting some alternative
jokes.explanation: Adult humor that works through implication and the ambiguous qualifier.

jokes.text: It's a lot like the 20th, except everybody's afraid and the stock market is much lower.
jokes.setup: Explaining the 21st century to the reanimated father
jokes.punchline: Summarizing modern life as fear and financial loss
jokes.explanation: Good social commentary disguised as exposition - captures early 2000s anxiety well.

jokes.text: Well I did finish first in the walk for the cure, of homosexuality!
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy claiming to be a healthy specimen
jokes.punchline: Revealing he participated in a walk for a 'cure' for homosexuality
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that plays on Comic Book Guy's character but ventures into problematic territory by today's standards.

jokes.text: Confucious? And Milton Berle? Boy I have been barking up the wrong tree!
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy seeing heaven as he dies
jokes.punchline: Surprised by the diverse mix of people in heaven, realizing his assumptions were wrong
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Comic Book Guy's narrow worldview being challenged even in death.

jokes.text: Nice posture. - Oh thank you! Mother always said: 'A curvy spine is the double roller coaster.'
jokes.setup: Compliment about posture
jokes.punchline: Bizarre maternal advice comparing spine curvature to amusement park rides
jokes.explanation: Absurdist humor through nonsensical but oddly poetic maternal wisdom.

jokes.text: Lost your spine, ha? You just keep finding new ways to disappoint me.
jokes.setup: Son has literally lost his spine to monster father
jokes.punchline: Father treating it as another personal failing rather than assault
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - abusive father blaming son for violence he committed. Perfect toxic relationship dynamic

jokes.text: You may keep my posterior, just please return the Jaba the Butt tattoo.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy negotiating for his body parts back
jokes.punchline: Pun on 'Jabba the Hutt' as 'Jaba the Butt' on his posterior
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific pun that works for Comic Book Guy's nerd interests and current situation.

jokes.text: You know Dr. Herschbach, our jobs are actually not that different. - I disagree.
jokes.setup: Actress comparing her job to Nobel Prize winner's
jokes.punchline: Scientist's immediate, flat disagreement
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response to Hollywood pretension. Excellent economy - maximum impact with minimal words.

jokes.text: I'm here to accept the Nobel Prize for stupid.
jokes.setup: Father dramatically arriving at Nobel ceremony
jokes.punchline: Claiming to accept Nobel Prize 'for stupid' - nonsensical category
jokes.explanation: Absurdist humor through the father's brain-damaged confusion of categories.

jokes.text: You all have such big hearts and such big brains with large juicy dripping with knowledge heads!
jokes.setup: Father giving what seems like a heartfelt speech
jokes.punchline: Speech turns increasingly disturbing as he focuses on harvesting organs
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from seemingly heartfelt to threatening, building tension for the violence to follow.

jokes.text: And I thought Halle Berry went nuts during her acceptance speech.
jokes.setup: Watching the increasingly violent Nobel ceremony
jokes.punchline: Reference to Halle Berry's emotional Oscar acceptance speech
jokes.explanation: Pop culture reference that works as comparison but feels somewhat cheap and disposable.

jokes.text: Muffins are suprisingly high in calories. The pyramids were actually built by Sears.
jokes.setup: Father claiming each brain reveals universe secrets
jokes.punchline: Mundane muffin fact followed by absurd pyramid conspiracy
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist pairing of mundane and ridiculous 'secrets.' The Sears pyramid theory is wonderfully silly.

jokes.text: This is more violent than the hip-hop worlds.
jokes.setup: Commenting on the violence at the Nobel ceremony
jokes.punchline: Comparing prestigious academic event unfavorably to hip-hop culture stereotypes
jokes.explanation: Weak joke that relies on tired stereotypes rather than clever observation or character work.

jokes.text: I maybe a soul, but I'm hungry. Can you throw in a little mazzard. Maybe a nice pizza pit(?)
jokes.setup: Father existing as a captured soul
jokes.punchline: Soul still having mundane earthly desires for food
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about the persistence of basic needs even in death/soul form.

jokes.text: Cool! Comics from the '70s. Superman vs Patty Hearst. Evel Knievel jumps the Jackson 5. Batman and Rhoda.
jokes.setup: Bart finding old comics
jokes.punchline: Absurd 1970s comic book titles mixing superheroes with period celebrities/events
jokes.explanation: Excellent period humor mixing superheroes with 70s culture. Each title gets progressively more absurd.

jokes.text: Wow, she looks like a background character in a Hanna-Barbera cartoon.
jokes.setup: Bart looking at the woman in the watch ad
jokes.punchline: Meta-comment about animation styles while being in an animated show
jokes.explanation: Good meta-joke about animation that acknowledges the medium without breaking the show's reality.

jokes.text: We can do anything we want! - Lets get really far ahead on our homework!
jokes.setup: Bart excited about unlimited possibilities with time control
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's lame suggestion to do homework
jokes.explanation: Perfect character contrast - Bart's mischievous potential versus Milhouse's nerd priorities.

jokes.text: Due to budget cuts, music class will now have a two drink minimum.
jokes.setup: School budget cut announcement
jokes.punchline: Turning elementary music class into a bar-like environment
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor about school budget problems. Great economy and quotability.

jokes.text: mayors... have... been... repeatedly...humiliated. Dammit!
jokes.setup: Mayor listing pranks affecting Springfield
jokes.punchline: Gets pranked himself while giving speech
jokes.explanation: Standard slapstick - predictable timing but solid execution

jokes.text: Come, let us kill them before learning of the magical secret which they possess!
jokes.setup: Angry mob discovering the pranksters
jokes.punchline: Illogical mob mentality wanting to kill first, ask questions later
jokes.explanation: Good satire of mob mentality and their self-defeating logic.

jokes.text: Yeah, but you say it first.
jokes.setup: Bart realizing they broke the watch
jokes.punchline: Milhouse repeating his earlier line about Bart saying things first
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows consistency in Milhouse's character while providing familiarity.

jokes.text: Well, I couldn't be happier, I'm the second coolest kid on earth.
jokes.setup: Milhouse thinking about being stuck in frozen time
jokes.punchline: His low standards for happiness - being second coolest
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse moment showing his low self-esteem and modest aspirations.

jokes.text: You know, Milhouse, I thought I'd love eating only frosting and giving the Pope wagging. But I miss being a regular kid in a real world.
jokes.setup: Bart reflecting on his time-stopped adventures
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of pranking the Pope alongside eating frosting
jokes.explanation: Good character growth moment for Bart with typical escalation to absurd mischief.

jokes.text: You know, Milhouse, I thought I'd love eating only frosting and giving the Pope wagging. But I miss being a regular kid in a real world.
jokes.setup: Bart reflecting on time-stopped adventures
jokes.punchline: Missing normalcy after ultimate freedom including papal harassment
jokes.explanation: Good character growth moment - Bart learning consequences of unlimited power

jokes.text: Boy, that took forever. - Yeah. Well, I work better in a structured environment.
jokes.setup: Complaining that learning watch repair took a long time
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's earnest explanation for why it took so long
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Milhouse's studious nature even in extreme circumstances.

jokes.text: Yeah. And I can't run too fast after 15 years of eating nothing but gummy worms. - I'm gonna quit tomorrow, I swear.
jokes.setup: Bart explaining why he can't run fast
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's addict-like promise to quit eating gummy worms
jokes.explanation: Good joke about addiction applied to something innocent, with typical broken promises.

jokes.text: Hum, why is Bart so tall? And shaggy? - Just one of life's mysteries. Like why is my nose jammed full of army men.
jokes.setup: Homer noticing Bart looks different after 15 years
jokes.punchline: Marge's casual acceptance and her own weird problem
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor with perfect Simpsons logic - treating major changes as minor mysteries.

jokes.text: Jealous much? Why can't I tinker with the fabric of existence? Let the baby have her bottle.
jokes.setup: Lisa being dismissed when she explains what happened
jokes.punchline: Her intellectual frustration escalating to existential complaints
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa moment showing her intelligence and frustration at being dismissed as a child.
